content.assessment: This proposal's cross-domain coordination logic is demographically well-grounded: it recognizes that school-readiness rules, rural network structure, and private/church growth interact with identification and segregation in ways that are spatially uneven. The record shows pronounced regional divergence in both child-population contraction and disadvantage concentration, and Hungary's historical choice to preserve small rural schools rather than consolidate constrains which anti-segregation mechanisms (e.g., mixing across parallel tracks) are structurally feasible in single-class schools.
opportunities: Coordination review could explicitly map policy interaction against the documented North-South/East-West divide and high-disadvantage-concentration settlements to prioritize intervention geography
strengths: Correctly identifies that adjacent policies (readiness rules, rural network structure) interact with SEN identification through documented channels, such as the 2020 readiness rule change's measurable effect on SNI growth
Coordination mechanism could account for the single-class rural school structural constraint that limits within-school anti-segregation tools in much of the rural network
threats: Middle-class exit to private/church schools is described as a trust-erosion dynamic not easily reversed by administrative coordination, and this dynamic may be spatially concentrated in ways that compound existing regional disadvantage patterns
Persistent institutional resistance combined with locked-in demographic contraction narrows the effective window for structural reform before smaller cohorts pass through the system
weaknesses: The single-class rural school stock, a demographic-structural legacy of non-consolidation, means several standard anti-segregation mechanisms (parallel-track balancing) may be mechanically inapplicable in a large share of small rural schools, requiring differentiated regional strategies not detailed in the proposal
